data.title: Alibaba: H200 Provides A Massive Growth Catalyst
data.description:
Shares of Alibaba (BABA) rose more than 5% on Thursday after the U.S. government cleared the path for H200 GPU shipments to China. The availability of new high-performing GPUs could accelerate Alibaba’s growth trajectory in the important Cloud division which has taken over as a major growth driver within Alibaba’s portfolio in recent years.

In the last fiscal half year, Cloud generated 30% year-over-year growth which has in large part been driven by accelerating adoption of AI products and services (such as Alibaba's Qwen chat assistant) within the AI Cloud platform. Further, investors clearly have a lot of appetite for Chinese Cloud investments right now which could make Alibaba an out-performance candidate in 2026. I like shares here, despite the higher valuation, and see an upside revaluation case for the company if current growth momentum persists.

Previous rating

I rated shares of Alibaba a strong buy -- Don't Worry About Margins -- because of the company’s momentum in the Cloud division, which benefited from increasing AI product and service adoption, and other growth initiatives, especially in the instant delivery business. Alibaba is now set to receive H200 AI-optimized GPUs from Nvidia (NVDA) which could be an accelerant for the company’s Cloud business. As a result, I expect a higher revenue growth rate for Alibaba as well as an increasing shift of revenues over to the Cloud business division.

H200 shipments is a key catalyst for Alibaba

Alibaba has suffered like other Chinese large-cap technology companies from a shortage of graphics processing units which are used in Chinese Data Centers to run AI applications. The U.S. government just changed its policy with regards to such GPU exports which opened up a pathway for the resumption of H200 GPU shipments to China. Since a shortage of GPUs has been a bottleneck problem within Alibaba’s Cloud intelligence platform, I believe that the resumption of GPU shipments could potentially accelerate the company’s growth in 2026 and beyond. Alibaba is allegedly preparing to import 200,000 H200 AI GPUs, assuming that Beijing will give the necessary approvals.

Past Cloud investments have paid off big time for Alibaba... as the segment posted a massive 30% upsurge in revenue in the first six months of the current fiscal year. Alibaba committed $53B over three years to Cloud and AI infrastructure which means the enterprise is prioritizing its Cloud business over its e-Commerce operations in terms of CapEx allocation.

With Cloud growing 2.5X faster than Alibaba's dominant e-Commerce operations, the tech platform is in a great position to sustain its current momentum already. With new H200 chips set to get integrated into Alibaba's AI infrastructure stack this year, the tech enterprise could potentially accelerate its Cloud growth even further, helping Alibaba to fetch a higher market valuation as well.

The H200 chip is a game-changer for Alibaba mainly because it offers the company a lever to up-grade its entire infrastructure stack. The H200 GPU is ~7X faster than the previously deployed H20 GPU which was a specifically designed AI chip with performance capped at approximately 296 TFLOPS in order to comply with U.S. export regulations.

The H200 GPU offers 1,979 TFLOPS of raw processing speed which makes the chip approximately 6.7X times faster than the H20… which will help Alibaba to drastically improve its Qwen 2.5 training performance, condense training timelines and enhance the value of Alibaba’s Cloud platform in general.

Beijing recently mandated regulations requiring Chinese companies to use at least 50% of domestic semiconductor manufacturing equipment when adding new production capacity. While this is set to boost the independence of the Chinese semiconductor industry in the long term, China’s largest tech enterprises still lag behind their U.S. rivals and depend on higher-performing U.S. GPUs from the U.S.

In fact, a shortage of GPUs is why companies like Baidu (BIDU) have turned to in-house chip production which the market has been increasingly willing to reward with a higher valuation multiplier. Baidu is also planning on bringing its chip business to the Hong Kong stock exchange, as I discussed here: Chip Unit IPO A Major Catalyst. With investor interest in Chinese chip plays like Baidu, Moore Threads or Biren soaring, I believe the resumption of chip exports to China could be a major catalyst for a higher valuation, and stronger Cloud performance for Alibaba, in 2026.

Narrowing valuation gap between Alibaba and U.S. Cloud peers

Shares of Alibaba are currently trading at a forward price-to-earnings ratio of 16.7X which makes the Chinese technology enterprise significantly cheaper than U.S. companies with large-scale Cloud businesses. Alibaba’s closest rival in the U.S. is Amazon (AMZN) which also has a sizable e-Commerce enterprise and owns the world’s largest public Cloud infrastructure platform AWS. Compared to Amazon, shares of Alibaba are still a complete bargain and are trading at only about 65% of Amazon’s earnings-based valuation.

In my last work on Alibaba I calculated a fair value of $188/share, based off of a 20.0X fair value P/E which I previously justified with the firm's broadening product pipeline, strategic growth initiatives in the instant delivery business and potential for Qwen, specifically in the enterprise market. In addition, recent momentum for AI businesses for new IPOs, like Biren and Moore Threads, show a strong investor appetite for tech platforms with AI and chip exposure. Therefore, I believe a $188 per-share valuation target may ultimately prove to be conservative for Alibaba.

While investors have applied steep valuation discounts in recent years to Chinese large-cap tech enterprises like Baidu and Alibaba, chiefly because of huge government interventions in the technology sector in 2020, the valuation gap between Alibaba and Amazon has significantly narrowed lately. This is due in part due to the significant boost Alibaba experienced in its Cloud business, following the roll-out of Qwen-based AI products in the last two years.

Risks with Alibaba

A risk for Alibaba, longer term, is that Beijing could demand an even higher-than-50% share of semiconductor equipment to be produced domestically, meaning China could potentially cut off foreign GPU manufacturers from its market entirely. What would change my mind about Alibaba specifically is if the tech enterprise were to fail to accelerate its growth in its important Cloud unit, driven by Qwen bot adoption, which is currently the single biggest growth driver for the tech company.

Final thoughts

The clearing of H200 GPU shipments to China could be a major moment in Alibaba’s history, specifically as it relates to the development and high-grading of the company’s Cloud infrastructure platform that is still heavily dependent on foreign-produced AI GPUs. While Beijing is making efforts in order to mandate domestically-sourced semiconductor supplies, China is likely going to continue to remain dependent on U.S.-sourced graphics processing units. This is good news for Nvidia, but also for Alibaba which could drastically upscale the performance of its Cloud platform. Higher training capacity and improved Qwen inference performance enhance the value of Alibaba’s entire AI infrastructure stack and could lead to higher Alibaba Cloud platform adoption rates, and potentially an acceleration of revenue growth in Alibaba’s Cloud business in 2026.

data.title: Energy Transfer: My Top 6 Reasons To Invest In The Partnership
data.description:
Introduction

As Energy Transfer (NYSE: ET) has been one of my largest holdings for 5 years, I want to go through the top reasons why an income-focused investor should buy into the company’s units. To recap, ET is one of the largest midstream companies in the world, boasting a vast and impressive infrastructure network that allows the company to dominate the energy industry.

Reason #1: The Distribution Fortress

Currently, Energy Transfer is paying a yield nearing 8%, while management is reaffirming its guidance for a 3% to 5% annual increase. Many could see this as a yield trap, but the payout is easily covered by its 1.9x DCF, meaning that the company is easily earning what it is paying out. Furthermore, even with the current state of the energy industry, the company is seeing record volumes through its network and thus cash flows, meaning that the company can easily self-fund all of its plans without needing to issue new units.

Reason #2: AI to Pipe

Right now, ET is no longer moving gas for stoves but is becoming the de facto utility for the current AI revolution, with various deals such as the LT agreement with CloudBurst Data Centers to supply 450,000MMBtu/d of natural gas. The company is also in active discussions with over 70 data center developers across various states. The strategy is smart as Data centers are directly bypassing the grid and going directly to ETs to secure gas instead of waiting 4 to 7 years. Another key point of this has been ET securing a massive 10-year agreement with Oracle (ORCL) that is hitting full stride this month, as ET will supply approximately 900,000 Mcf/d of natural gas. ET is utilizing its Texas intrastate network to deliver this gas, which offers higher margins than interstate lines as it is less regulated. These kinds of deals have a premium as data centers are not looking for cheap gas but reliable gas, meaning that these operators are willing to pay large demand charges to ensure that even during a winter freeze, the gas won’t be diverted.

Reason #3: Current Projects

Right now, Mustang Draw I & II are the dual engines driving the company’s processing expansion in 2026 and are designed to solve one of the Permian’s biggest issues: Associated Gas. I’ll do a bit of explaining. Processing is the high-margin bridge between a wellhead and a long-haul pipeline in which Mustang Draw will increase ET’s ability to capture margins. Mustang I will have a capacity of 275 MMcf/d that will enter service in Q2 2026, while Mustang II will have a capacity of 250 MMcf/d entering service in Q4 2026. This will solve the associated gas issue, as many drillers in the Permian don’t have a place to process the gas and have to flare it or slow down oil production. The plan will have to vertically integrate all of the natural gas value chain and operate as a toll booth model, ensuring the same drilling pace.

Following my piece on Matador Resources (MTDR), there is a lot of potential with the Hugh Brinson Pipeline as well, in my opinion, as it can act as a bottleneck breaker for the company. This is in order to connect the Waha Hub in West Texas to Maypearl to boost prices, where the region generally has a lack of takeaway capacity. Hence, many producers are in huge demand for such a pipeline to have better pricing. The pipeline’s first phase is set to be completed by the end of the year and will move around 1.5 Bcf/d. The demand is so immense, considering that sometimes Waha could be at $0 while it is at $3.5 on the Gulf Coast.

Reason #4: The Lake Charles LNG Suspension

ET made a major pivot in late December and has suspended development of its Lake Charles LNG facility. The project has been in the works for a decade, driven by the opportunities that LNG provided. Yet for many, this was a shock, but I believe that this has maybe been one of the smartest moves by management in years, and this directly shows that this is no longer the growth at any cost ET but a company focused on Risk-Adjusted Returns. The company had trouble finding partners for the remaining 50% of the project, and rather than stepping up and funding the larger portion of the $15B project itself and spiking the company’s leverage at a tumultuous time for the Oil & Gas industry, ET decided to stop the project completely. This period is why the market is rewarding reliability over commodity price exposure.

Hugh Brinsom, for example, will provide mid-teens returns with no direct commodity risk that will pay back itself in 5 to 7 years, while the LNG plant with all the other LNG projects coming online might find diminishing returns and take 15 years to break even. The wave of supply is simply too big a risk for the company to take on such an endeavor with Qatar and existing US projects ramping up and hitting the market past 2026, meaning that there might be price compression in the future. This will ultimately remove the overhang of a potential multi-billion dollar debt raise and allow the company to keep its leverage target intact.

Reason #5: The Valuation Gap

ET is currently experiencing a valuation gap compared to its large peers, as it has the lowest EV/EBITDA of the bunch at 9.07 and its price to DCF is at around 6.5x. If we directly compare it to the gold standard of stability, Enterprise Products Partners, the company could be re-rated by over 20% while having the same leverage ratio. ET also pays a current yield of nearly 8% while EPD is at 6.8%.

So why does the Gap exist? I believe that this is mainly from the perception of previous investors that ET prioritizes aggressive M&A over returning cash to unitholders, and it used to have leverage concerns. With what I mentioned earlier in the analysis, we could assess that this is no longer the case, hence why the company should be more akin to the top of the industry. We should see the gap closing in 2026 as the company is hitting on all marks that should trigger a market re-rate, such as the leverage milestone, as well as the AI growth narrative that changes the perceived slow-growth of ET. Management has also signaled that unit buybacks are on the table as the company has finished funding its $5.5B CapEx plan and has a vast trove of disposable cash flows.

Reason #6: Growth

2026 is set to be a great year in terms of EBITDA growth for the company as management has guided for $17.3B to $17.7B in EBITDA, which would result in a near 10% growth over 2025 at the top of the range, which is extraordinary for such a company. This would be thanks to all organic projects coming online that are set to become cash cows, mainly Hugh Brinson and the Mustang Draw, but also the company realizing full synergies from WTG and Crestwood Equity Partners. If the company were valued at the same EV/EBITDA and had the growth expected at the top line, the company would have a price per unit of around $22.36, factoring in the current distribution yield, a capital appreciation of 32.4% and a total potential return of 40.3% based on the current price of $16.89 per unit.

Risks

While not a risk, I believe that it is important for investors to understand the differences between investing in a C-Corp and an MLP. I dived into the matter in one of my previous ET analyses, here if you want to check it out.

Right now, the oil market is facing an oversupply that has pressured prices downwards. And while ET is not technically dependent directly on oil prices, its customers and suppliers are. If prices drop too far, production might stall, thus the company might have less volume through its system. This has been the current dynamic for E&P independent producers that have been discontent with the US push for Venezuelan oil.

The AI narrative is a big tailwind to invest in the partnership, but as ET is in talks with over 70 projects, these are not yet signed contracts. If tech companies face a correction in 2026 based on the speculative nature of AI, many of these capital-intensive data centers might get canceled or deferred.

Conclusion

I believe that ET is a staple for any income-focused investors as it currently arbors one of the better risk-adjusted yields while being significantly undervalued compared to the market and even its peers. 2026 will be a great year for Energy Transfer and its unitholder, and in that sense, I keep my Strong Buy rating on the company.

data.title: Sandisk Is Free-Soloing At The Top -- Beware Falling ASPs
data.description:
This isn't the best entry point for Sandisk (SNDK) stock as a medium-term investor, though as a short-term momentum trader it could work. Personally, I don't like the aggressive cyclicality of the trade this late in the cycle. It's clear that Sandisk stock probably has about 9 months left to run before consolidation and decline begins.

This isn't an AI-memory "bubble" in the speculative sense; there's a real volume-led fundamental improvement underway, and the expectation from the market is that AI-driven enterprise SSD and data-center NAND flash demand meaningfully lifts the NAND demand floor. For this to be sustainable, both demand and supply will need to hold a strong relationship over the medium term.

In Q1 FY26, total exabytes were at +31% year-over-year, and total average selling prices ("ASP") per gigabyte ("GB") were at -9% year-over-year. The market is pricing in future price increases ahead of time, most likely manifesting in Q2 and beyond.

Growing data centers exponentially is not realistic—it would break the economy without tangible and equal exponential return on investment. In Q1 FY26, Sandisk's data center revenue was actually -10% year-over-year, because data center exabytes were +1% year-over-year in the face of ASP/GB down -11% year-over-year. The total growth was accumulated in Edge and Consumer end markets, where growth was +30% year-over-year and +27% year-over-year, respectively.

What we can say is this: AI likely raises the floor somewhat for enterprise SSD demand due to an increase in retrieval, caching, and vector stores, but Q1 FY26 mix does not yet validate the thesis fully. It's completely logical that the coming cyclicality will not be as brutal as otherwise, but Sandisk will have to continue a pivot to AI-focused and data-center led memory, of which it is currently not as well positioned for as SK Hynix, Samsung (SSNLF), and Micron (MU).

NAND Supply & CAPEX Demand

NAND prices always face a ceiling because NAND supply grows in two basic ways; either companies can make more chips with the factories they already have, or they can build more factories. Prices for NAND have been rising, and so manufacturers respond by boosting how much data each chip can store and by expanding equipment and factories. Almost always, oversupply takes place of undersupply eventually—forming a "glut."

Micron, one of Sandisk's primary competitors, has stated that “industry demand is greater than supply for both DRAM and NAND," and reference NAND node plans. When large suppliers see demand higher than supply and guide to margin expansion, capacity usually accelerates and pressures NAND pricing.

Logically, broad AI capex from hyperscalers could slow as return on investment is optimized, and when this occurs, Sandisk's ramped capacity could force a structural de-rating in its stock (prices collapsing due to oversupply) before the next leg starts again. This is almost physical in its manifestation, like a chain reaction of greed to excess to fear to deficiency, and then back to geed. After all, that is the economic cycle, and it applies directly to the memory-chip boom.

Sandisk's Q1 FY26 revenue mix was about 60% Edge, 28% Consumer, and 12% Datacenter. The top 10 customers were about 40% of revenue, versus 53% a year ago. You must be aware that the company's current mix is much more heavily in cyclical and price sensitive areas (Edge and Consumer) with Datacenter only a small element. This undoubtedly raises the risk versus Micron, which has data center sales at about 56% of revenue.

Price Target

Consensus looks quite accurate when it comes to Sandisk, so I am presenting the Wall Street estimates here as it aligns with my macro view of capex digestion in 2028 which could lead to a broader economic consolidation period alongside equity market valuation compression—or a minor recession.

We have eight analysts for FY28, so the consensus estimate is somewhat reliable and can be used as a parameter for my independent price target. June 2028 EPS at 22 with a forward P/E of 15 in June 2027 leads to a bullish outcome of $330/share in June 2027, after the price potentially reaches forward P/E of 20 in June 2026 and EPS of $24 in June 2027 ($480/share). So the six-month upside is about 50% before a major reversal back toward the entry price. That's not a bad trade but it's a bit late and very short-term, so I am rating Sandisk a Hold and will not be buying it.

Q2 Earnings Expectations

In Q2, management is expecting revenue up 38.5% year-over-year. Q2 could very well see double-digit price increases in my opinion, but bear in mind this is already priced into the stock so it would simply be confirming strength. Additionally mid-single-digit bit growth is likely. Compared to Q1 this is good, as there were revenue growth dynamics that included ASP declines; equity sentiment should become more steady through 2026 for Sandisk as result. However, it's very prone to equity sentiment weakness in 2027 before the real structural "break" in 2028.

Conclusion: Hold

Maybe you are like Alex Honnold (the man in the analysis cover photo) and can trade memory-based semiconductor stocks with elite short-term discipline. After all, he climbs 914m mountains without any ropes. It may be wise to use a stop-loss, because climbing the memory-chip mountain near the peak without ropes could have you out of a lot of your principal—and fast. This is a six-month trade before ice makes the mountain too risky to grasp, so don't be reckless out there.

Sandisk is experiencing a structural re-rating based on rising NAND ASPs, but that doesn't mean that a glut isn't on its way. It's always darkest before the dawn, and it's always the most beautiful just before the sun goes down—similarly, the best entry points in the stock market come before others have woken up, and you'd want to get out well before the party ends and you have nobody to sell to.

I've heard it so many times before: "don't time the market," except the best long-term capital allocators always secretly do, they just don't guess based on speculative excess. The meaning behind their risk-on/risk-off posture is all in the simple macro valuations.

data.title: Visteon: High-Performance Compute Transition Bridging The China Gap
data.description:
Founded in 2000 as a spin-off from Ford, Visteon (VC) is a US-based tier 1 automotive supplier focusing on the cockpit electronics and electrification segments serving the global markets. Today, it considers global OEMs like Renault, Volvo, and Chevrolet as customers. More recently, VC has announced a strategic transformation to position itself as an SDV (software-defined vehicle) platform provider.

Despite the choppy share performance since going public and post-bankruptcy restructuring in the 2010s, VC appears to have seen quite a momentum over the past year. Today it trades at $104 per share, up 26% on a 1-year basis. Much of that has to do with the over 9.4% surge just on a YTD basis. With the stock down by more than 25% if we zoom out over the past five years, VC appears to be in an early stage of rebuilding the progress to its past valuation today, leveraging an AI play.

I initiate my coverage with a buy rating. My 1-year price target of $117.5 presents a 13% upside from the current price of $104 per share.

Financial Reviews

Overall, fundamentals as of Q3 2025 remain fairly robust. In Q3, revenue was $917 million, a 6.4% YoY decline. Yet, it was merely caused by temporary headwinds, such as unplanned downtime at Jaguar Land Rover (JLR), rather than a structural issue. All that aside, the underlying technology remains in high demand, in my view.

Profitability-wise, VC performed well despite the 6% top-line decline, displaying a good operational excellence as of Q3. Adjusted EBITDA of $119 million means that VC realized a 13% margin, maintaining the same performance as last year. However, it is important to note that while VC’s cost discipline has relatively been working, persisting VC’s lower battery management system (BMS) volume that is supposed to bring in higher margins due to the slower EV market in North America could potentially be a leading indicator for future contraction, in my opinion.

Balance sheet and liquidity are strong. VC ended Q3 2025 with $765 million in cash and equivalents with around $288 million of long-term debt, suggesting a roughly net cash position of $477 million, a significant improvement already over past year, when net cash was roughly $325 million in the beginning of 2025. Liquidity-wise, I consider the $292 million of operating cash flow (OCF) to be strong, while the $88 million use of cash for investing for CAPEX is controlled. In Q3, VC also paid its first-ever quarterly dividend of $0.25 per share as well as share repurchases.

Catalyst

I believe there are two interesting medium to long-term catalysts for VC. The first one is VC’s launch of SmartCore HPC (High-Performance Computing) platform just this week at CES 2026. Given the relatively higher magnitude of the platform upgrade which claims to be able to do 700 trillion operations per second, I feel that this move may mark VC’s decisive transition from simply being an auto hardware supplier to a serious AI-compute platform company, creating the basis for a potential re-rating for the stock, in my view.

As of today, VC’s P/S multiple has been trading within the range of 0.8x to 1x, which is the similar range to that of one of its competitors in the digital cockpit segment, Aptiv (APTV). I believe a re-rating could drive VC's P/S expansion to a level beyond such a range in the next twelve months.

Furthermore, another potential key catalyst for VC in 2026 could be its recent momentum in China-related activities as of late. While in 2025 we see how VC’s customers such as JLR-branded segment losing market share to domestic OEM brands in China driving lower sales, I believe that the situation may be a bit different in 2026.

I think the inflection point here is that VC has recently secured contracts with fast-growing and leading Chinese OEMs such as Geely’s Zeekr brand in addition to Chery, suggesting a well-acceptance of its technology in the market, potentially more than offset the 2025 losses. The China opportunity is also reinforced by VC’s investment in domestic capability, with the new R&D facility in Wuhan to come online in 2026. On that note, I believe VC is in a good position to receive an updated view by the market that its China exposure is a long-term opportunity rather than geographical risk.

Risk

Overall, I believe risk remains moderate. In addition to rising geopolitical trade barriers in China or supply chain disruptions, I think that there is always a possibility of VC facing slippage and delay in its significant leap from digital cockpit displays into HPC. In my opinion, the common drawback here may have to do with software instability which could lead to missing revenue targets and also escalating R&D labor costs, further discounting VC’s positive re-rating opportunities.

Valuation / Pricing

My target price for VC is driven by the following assumptions for the bull vs bear scenarios of the FY 2026 projection, consistent with consensus estimates:

  1. Bull scenario (60% probability) assumptions - Successful shift from a traditional auto hardware supplier to an AI-compute cockpit platform. The China domestic OEM wins with Geely and others more than offset the downturn in the western OEM brand segment like JLR. P/S multiple to expand beyond 1x, higher than its peer APTV.

  2. Bear scenario (40% probability) assumptions - VC is facing some software stability or chip supply chain issues with its SmartCore HPC. Furthermore, the macro issue also results in a sticky North American EV Winter, which results in the BMS segment underperforming for another year. The stock remains stuck in an auto supplier trap, with the multiple compressing to 0.6x P/S, lower than where it is trading today.

Consolidating all the information above into my model, I arrived at an FY 2026 weighted target price of $117.5 per share, or around $117.75 per share if we account for the $0.275 per share dividend, presenting a potential upside of roughly 13% from the current level. At this point, I would give the stock a buy rating.

Conclusion

VC enters FY 2026 transitioning from a hardware-centric cockpit display to an AI-compute HPC platform provider. I believe that the 2026 thesis hinges on the successful commercialization of the SmartCore HPC platform, steady North American revenue base, and the stronger presence in China through its Wuhan R&D hub to capture more Chinese domestic OEM share, which may continue to grow against the Western counterpart. Risk remains moderate. Any delays due to chip supply constraint or software instability could reset the multiple re-rating opportunity. Nonetheless, I feel slightly bullish on VC overall, and that is why I assigned a 60-40 bull-bear probabilities in my target price analysis, suggesting that VC appears attractively priced today.

data.title: Lumexa Imaging: Resilient Demand With Compounding Scale Moat
data.description:
Investment overview

I give a buy rating for Lumexa Imaging, Inc. (LMRI) because it enjoys resilient demand for outpatient imaging, demographic tailwinds, and also a compounding scale advantage as it grows.

Business description

LMRI owns and operates outpatient diagnostic imaging centers, which focus only on imaging. It runs Magnetic Resonance Imaging [MRI], Computed Tomography [CT], and Positron Emission Tomography [PET] scanners in outpatient centers outside hospitals. As of Q3 2025, LMRI runs 184 centers across 13 states, making it the second-largest player in the US. Of these, it owns 99 centers and operates another 85 through joint ventures [JV] with large hospitals.

The Secular Shift to Outpatient Settings

I believe the main growth driver for LMRI is the shift of patients away from hospitals toward freestanding imaging centers. And this is happening for a simple reason: hospital imaging is too expensive. For reference, the same MRI can cost much more in a hospital than in a freestanding center, often by ~ 60%, and this has pushed insurers to opt for outpatient imaging. Insurers are also making efforts to drive this shift by paying the same rate regardless of where the scan is done and making hospital scans harder to approve. This naturally steers patients toward lower-cost centers like those LMRI operates.

Aside from cost, I expect consumers to prefer freestanding imaging centers because they are faster and more convenient. Unlike hospitals, they focus only on scans, which simplifies scheduling and reduces wait times. They are also easier to access, quicker to check into, and less likely to face delays (i.e., they don’t need to compete against scans from hospital emergencies). For LMRI, all these dynamics mean it doesn’t need to spend a lot on marketing to capture traffic (since insurers want lower costs and patients want convenience).

There is also a bullish trend that favors LMRI, and that is the aging population. As the US population ages, demand for imaging related to chronic illness, cancer, and neurological care naturally goes up. And since hospitals are already facing capacity and staffing constraints, they simply cannot absorb this additional volume. This is where LMRI steps in and fills this gap by handling imaging outside the hospital.

The Joint Venture Moat

LMRI’s joint venture model is something I see as a strong moat. Many readers would just see it as just a partnership, but it is more than that. In imaging, the hardest part is not buying scanners. It is getting steady patient referrals. By running 85 centers with large hospitals, LMRI manages to lock in referral flow from doctors inside those systems. Take the case when LMRI enters a market with a partner like UPMC; it immediately starts with demand already in place instead of competing for patients.

This joint venture also has very positive aspects from a financial perspective. A sub-scale imaging operator usually cannot negotiate good payment rates with large insurers such as UnitedHealthcare or Anthem. But when LMRI negotiates alongside a major hospital, it can get better reimbursement rates than standalone imaging operators. This helps to support higher margins. And once LMRI gets integrated into a hospital’s referral and IT systems, LMRI becomes hard to replace since it is well embedded into the workflows. Doctors are already set up to refer patients to LMRI, and scheduling and billing systems are fully connected. When this happens, switching to another imaging operator would require retraining staff, changing systems, and disrupting daily workflows. Hence, my belief is because of this, hospitals rarely switch providers, creating high switching costs and making it difficult for competitors to enter.

Scale advantage compounds

LMRI's competitive advantage vs. smaller players compound over time as it scales, and the logic is straightforward. Each year’s new centers add more EBITDA as they mature with little incremental capital needed since the cost of the building is fixed, the labor cost is also fixed, and they need to spend little on marketing. This essentially allows LMRI to spit out a lot more free cash flow over time as it gets bigger. This gives LMRI flexibility to open more via organic CAPEX or acquisition. When acquisition valuations are high, LMRI can simply shift to rely more on its own builds rather than overpaying. There is also economics of scale at the consolidated level. As LMRI adds more centers, it is able to amortize costs like corporate staff and systems across a larger store base, and that in turn lowers cost per center and lifts overall margins.

Valuation

Using the management's long-term growth guide of 5% to 7%, midpoint 6%, I forecast LMRI to achieve revenue of $1.15 billion in FY28. Adj. EBITDA margin was 21.2% in FY24, and that should go up since there is fixed cost leverage. Say LMRI scales up to its long-term margin target (22% to 23% range) by FY28, using 23%; that equates to an FY28 adj. EBITDA of ~$264.5 million. With this growth profile, it makes sense for LMRI to trade at a discount to RadNet (RDNT), which has a ~10+% growth outlook (RDNT trades at 18x and LMRI trades at 12x). But for that minor growth difference, I wonder if such a big discount is warranted. That said, suppose LMRI trades at 12x forward adj. EBITDA multiple; LMRI is worth ~$3.2 billion in enterprise value in FY27. Take away the net debt of ~$750 billion (post-IPO), and LMRI should be worth a market cap of $2.45 billion.

Risk

The main risk for LMRI is pressure on scan volumes and payments. Imaging centers rely on doctors for referrals, so demand can fall sharply if referrals slow or new centers ramp up more slowly. Since costs like rent, staff, and MRI equipment are mostly fixed, even a small drop in volume can lead to a steep decline in profits.

Conclusion

I am positive on LMRI because of its resilient demand and organic growth drivers. If this plays out as I expected, with revenue and earnings growing as per management's long-term guidance, even if multiples don’t expand, the upside is still attractive.

data.title: Badger Meter: Deserving Of An Upgrade Following A Healthy Value Contraction (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Thesis review

In June of last year, I wrote an article covering Badger Meter (BMI) with the title "Priced to Perfection." In the article I had a bearish take on this name, simply stating that the earnings multiple was too high and it undermined the impact of tariffs and their short-term impact on the bottom line. At the time of my writing, BMI traded for a PE ratio of 50x, while its closest competitor, Xylem (XYL), was trading for less than 35x. Other than the tariff risks and the premium valuation, nothing on the company's financials signaled any imminent concerns, but these two risks were concerning enough to the market that BMI stock declined by 31% since my writing, and Xylem, which I had a neutral stance on, appreciated by 11.43%.

Looking at YoY return, BMI lost 20.74% of value, and Itron (ITRI) lost 11%, massively underperforming Xylem, which returned 21.86%, beating the S&P return by 3.34 percentage points. Quoting Ben Graham, in the short term the market is a voting machine, and in the long term it's a weighing machine. The BMI stock decline was simply due to the market releasing it might have overbought on this quality company, and the multiple was bound for correction. The firm's fundamentals remain solid, and this short-term price fluctuation is a mere reflection of the market's irrationality. Right now the earnings multiple appears to have contracted to healthy levels, making me confident to upgrade this name to a hold.

2011 period vs Now

When reading the latest earnings transcript, one major takeaway was the investors' worry about the risk that the firm's organic growth might be peaking. The concern is coming from the fact that there has been noise that the water utility funding gap continues to grow, since that has a direct impact on BMI revenue. Management did state that very little funding from ARPA went to metering, and according to McKinsey research, in 2024 the water utility funding gap was estimated to be roughly $110 billion and projected to reach $194 billion in 2030.

The reason a historical reference matters here is because, during the 2011s period when the US market was recovering from the global recession, underinvestments in water utilities and metering resulted in BMI's topline decline. The CEO did state that given that the company now predominantly sells ultrasonic meters instead of mechanical meters, ultrasonic meters' reliance on batteries as a power source acts as a forcing function for a quick replacement cycle once the battery lifespan has been reached.

The CEO's point is validated by the revenue chart below, which shows that during 2008-2011, topline revenue ebbed and flowed, netting stagnation in that period. However, in recent years, growth has been exponential, proving high replacement cycles. Going forward, management expects the base revenue growth rate to normalize at high single digits, which is a healthy growth rate given its scale and the industry it's operating in.

Financial Analysis

Q3 2025 Income Statement

For the latest quarter, BMI grew revenue 13% to $235.66 million, which resulted in an estimate beat by $3.77 million. Excluding contribution from SmartCover, base revenue grew by 8%, bolstered by improving sales mix. Cost of revenue increased 12% to $139.82 million, resulting in gross profits increasing 14% to $95.84 million and landing gross margins at 40.6%, improving 50 bps from a prior-year quarter. Operating expenses increased by 15% to $49.75 million, leading to operating income of $46.08 million (13% YoY increase) and operating margins of 19.6%, up 10 bps from 19.5% YoY. When excluding SmartCover, operating profit increased by 15% YoY and margins expanded by 120 bps to 20.75%. I expect this drag on margins as a result of the acquisition to be transient once cost synergies fully materialize. Management did state that it wasn't expecting the acquisition to be value accretive immediately; instead, value is expected to be realized in year 2.

Interest income decreased by 41%, resulting in pretax income increasing 11% to $47.5 million. Income tax increased by 14% and led to net income increasing 9% to $35.08 million (landing net margins at 14.88%) and EPS increasing 10% to $1.19, beating estimates by $0.10. For the fourth quarter, analysts estimate Q4 revenue of $232.30 million, implying a 14.7% YoY quarterly increase. If this estimate gets hit, FY 2025 revenue would sit at $928.25 million, 10.96% higher than FY 2024 revenue of $826.56 million. I consider these estimates to be fairly reflective of management's expectation of growth normalizing at single digits.

9 Months 2025 Income Statement

Over a 9-month period, revenue grew 12% to $695.95 million, and gross profits grew 17% to $289.08 million, with margins landing at 41.5%. This margin expansion illustrates the firm's efficiency in mitigating the impact of tariffs; management even raised the gross margin expectation from the historical range of 38%-40% to 39%-42%. Operating and pretax income grew 18% and 11%, landing margins at 20.17% and 20.69%, respectively. Net income increased 15% to $108.06 million, and net margins landed at 15.5%.

When looking at the broader margin profile, we can see that BMI had a healthy margin expansion since 2017; gross margins grew by 2.56 pps, EBIT margins grew by 6.12 pps, net margins grew by 6.81 pps, and FCF margins grew by 9.84 pps. This trend shows that ultrasonic meters earn a higher margin, and given the continuing revenue mix and a growing recurring revenue from its SaaS services, I expect more expansion going forward.

9 Months 2025 Cash Flow Statement

Looking at the cash flow statement, we can see that the 42% increase in SBC slightly impacted earnings, reporting mid-teen growth, but when adjusting for cash add-backs, operating and free cash flow increased 25% to $128.90 million and $112.41 million, respectively. The increase was offset by a cash outflow of $11.44 million from working capital.

Q3 2025 Balance Sheet

BMI has a healthy balance sheet; one concern is the surge in deferred taxes (increasing by 371% YoY) and other current liabilities. The settlement of these liabilities could have a short-term impact on earnings, which could result in lower EPS and an estimate miss. Given that stock prices have recently been more sensitive to earnings beats/misses announcements, the earnings impact that might be caused by these liability settlements shouldn't be overlooked. Despite a 22% YoY decline, BMI has enough cash to fund more acquisitions and continue to do and grow dividend payouts, thanks to maintaining its debt levels at zero.

Quant ratings

The quant rating maintains a hold on this name despite the valuation upgrade from an F to a D-; this is because growth downgraded from a B+ to a C, momentum downgraded from a B- to a D+, and revisions downgraded from a B to a C+, while the profitability factor remained steady at B+.

Valuation

As I have stated earlier in this article and in my previous coverage, my main criticism of Badger Meter has been the inflated earnings multiple that reached 53x at its peak; the multiple has since contracted to healthy levels at 36.84, in line with Xylem and slightly above its sector median at 33.38. Now given that BMI has a superior business model compared to Xylem and most of its peers, I think this current multiple is fair.

Conclusion

One impending risk investors should take notice of is that, even though BMI's multiple has contracted by a fair amount in my opinion, 36.84 PE is still rich, and the multiple could further contract from here, so I wouldn't flip my bearish sentiment to a buy as of yet. My initial take that BMI premium valuation should correct has played out well, and given that BMI continues to be a quality company with enough cash to fund inorganic growth and dividends, I think an upgrade to a hold is warranted.

data.title: Cushman & Wakefield: Resilient Growth Profile And Balance Sheet Deleveraging
data.description:
Summary

I give a buy rating on Cushman & Wakefield (CWK) because it has a resilient demand profile, exposure to the strong AI/data center tailwind, and is working hard to deleverage its balance sheet.

Company overview

CWK is a global real estate services company. It has ~52,000 employees operating in 60 countries and manages ~5.1 billion square feet of property worldwide. The business has four core activities: leasing, capital markets, services, and valuation. Leasing earns commissions by matching tenants with landlords, while capital markets generate larger but more cyclical fees from property sales and financing. Services provide recurring income through day-to-day building operations, and valuation supports banks and investors by providing required property assessments for reporting and financing.

Resilient demand

CWK's resilient demand profile is its biggest strength. Despite negative headlines around office vacancies, CWK has reported 8 consecutive quarters of leasing fee revenue growth y/y. This tells me CWK is actively gaining market share, and I expect this share gain momentum to continue for a simple reason. When interest rates go up, borrowing becomes expensive and real estate transactions slow. Boutique brokers (small, independent real estate brokerage firms) tend to struggle in this environment because they rely almost entirely on commissions from real estate transaction deals. When fewer transactions occur, revenue gets hit while fixed costs like rent and salaries remain. Hence, these boutique brokers typically get their profits squeezed during tough times, and by nature of their size, they typically don’t have a very strong balance sheet that can help them tide things through.

CWK is different. Because it has multiple core activities, especially earning recurring fees from managing buildings, it has a stable profit flowing in even when transaction activity slows, and that gives it the financial dry powder to pursue transactions aggressively (maybe pricing lower, better marketing, etc.) to gain market share. And note that these recurring service fees are very sticky in nature.

In addition, there is a structural tailwind that favors CWK. Large clients now prefer to work with partners that can provide advanced data and technology (such as systems that track energy use and automate regulatory reporting) as they help to lower costs and reduce compliance risk. As you can imagine, building and maintaining these systems is expensive, and most boutique brokers cannot afford them. Since CWK has the scale (i.e., the data) and balance sheet, it can offer such services, and I think this is another reason why CWK will continue to win market share.

Data center

CWK is also shifting its focus toward data centers, where I expect investment spending to grow in great magnitude in the coming years because of AI demand. Per management estimates, the addressable market is estimated to be $6.7 trillion in global data center spending by 2030. I think CWK has a good shot to capture a good piece of the pie here because it is able to service the asset's entire life cycle. For instance, CWK can help clients to find land, advise on financing, manage construction, and even support operations throughout the life cycle. This is a very strong value proposition for enterprises that want to have their own data center but are not experts in this field (unlike the hyperscalers, which are very sophisticated on this front). As more capital flows into this space, it should gradually ease CWK dependence on traditional office markets and rely more on a strong secular tailwind (AI and data centers).

Valuation and Balance Sheet

CWK balance sheet improvements make the equity story even better. Over the past 24 months, CWK has repaid $500 million of debt principal and laid out a path to reach net leverage of 2x by 2028 (3.2x now). If CWK can really get there, I see potential for it to achieve an investment-grade rating (which management is gunning for). That would change a lot of things. It doesn’t just mean that CWK will enjoy a lower cost of financing; it should also drive up the valuation multiple since there is less risk (more earnings flow to equity shareholders). Saying that CWK repays debt at the same pace implies total debt repayment of ~$750 million, which would bring total debt down to ~$2.4 billion in FY28, or a net debt of $1.8 billion. The consensus FY28 adj. EBITDA estimate is ~$856 million, implying a net leverage ratio of 2.1x (close enough to 2x). Note that this is assuming the pace of debt paydown doesn’t increase.

Coupling the fundamentals and path towards a cleaner balance sheet, it is interesting to learn that CWK trades at ~8.6x 2025 EBITDA, while its closest peer, CBRE Group (CBRE), trades at ~15x. Although it is fair to also say that CWK has a smaller scale (LTM revenue of $10 billion vs. CBRE with ~$39 billion), and CWK deserves some form of discount, this level of discount is too much. Both companies compete in the same global markets and sell the same services. If multiple simply re-rates to ~11x EBITDA (CBRE -1 standard deviation multiple over the past 3 years), the upside is meaningful.

Valuation math: 11x forward EBITDA * ~$725 million FY26e adj. EBITDA = $7.9 billion enterprise value, which equals a market cap of ~$5.5 billion (this is assuming no debt paydown, by the way).

Investment Risk

The plan to reduce net debt to 2x may not happen. If interest rates stay high, refinancing costs could eat into free cash flow and slow deleveraging. That would weaken the multiple's upside. The other risk is execution. My bull thesis depends on technology, efficiency gains, and CWK winning deals in the data center space. If these don’t materialize, or if the office market faces a permanent decline rather than a temporary slowdown, valuation will get impacted.

Conclusion

I give CWK a buy rating, as it is trading at a steep discount vs. its closest peer despite servicing the same markets and competing at a scaled level. If CWK can continue to show growth despite the slowdown in the office space and deleverage its balance sheet, I expect valuation multiples to re-rate higher.

data.title: Dynagas LNG: A Speculative Long Position Could Be Justified
data.description:
Introduction

Dynagas LNG Partners (DLNG) is a shipping company (technically it's an LP, but I will use the word 'company' to refer to Dynagas) operating a fleet of six LNG carriers. All carriers are currently employed with a weighted average charter term of 5.2 years with a contractual backlog in excess of $800M, but three of the vessels will have to be redeployed in 2028. The strong cash flows have allowed the company to rapidly reduce its net debt, while one series of preferred stock was retired last summer.

Robust Results, and This Will Continue for the Next Two Years

While we still have to wait for the company to report on its final quarter of 2025, considering the vessels have been chartered out at known rates, I don’t expect any major changes.

In the third quarter of 2025, the total revenue remained virtually unchanged at almost $39M. Meanwhile, the operating expenses increased slightly, mainly due to higher voyage expenses, partially reduced by slightly lower vessel operating expenses. This resulted in an operating income of approximately $18.8M, which is approximately $1M lower than in the third quarter of 2024.

This didn’t have a major impact on the bottom-line result, as the net interest expenses continued to decrease. That being said, the 50% increase in net income, which jumped to $18.7M, or $0.48 per share, was mainly caused by an increase in the ‘other income,’ which jumped to $5.2M. This was related to the income from insurance claims for damages incurred in prior years and should be seen as a non-recurring item.

As the company barely has any capital expenditures (the capex bill tends to be zero in most quarters), the net free cash flow, of course, exceeds the net income. As you can see below, the reported operating cash flow was $26.5M (including a net cash investment in the working capital position), while no capital expenditures were incurred.

This also means the net free cash flow was $26.5M, which is in excess of 40% higher than the reported net income, due to the difference between depreciation and amortization expenses and the actual incurred capex.

Note: this does not include the preferred dividend payments yet. The sole remaining series that’s still outstanding is the fixed-rate 9% preferred stock (which I will discuss later in this article). As there are 3M preferred shares outstanding, the quarterly preferred dividends are just $1.7M. So even after isolating the non-recurring ‘other income’ from the equation, it goes without saying the preferred dividends are still very well covered. In fact, as Dynagas retired its Series B preferred stock, the payout ratio is now even lower than before.

And despite retiring the preferred stock, the balance sheet remains very healthy, both from the perspective of the net debt level, as well as the total amount of equity. Although Dynagas does pay a distribution on its common shares, the majority of its income is retained on the balance sheet. And despite retiring $55M in preferred stock, the total amount of equity on the balance sheet decreased by just $23M in the first nine months of 2025.

With almost $35M in cash on the balance sheet, the liquidity remains strong, and Dynagas recently increased its distribution to $0.05 per quarter per share. This was predicted by fellow author Nikolaos Sismanis in his September article,

The one caveat going forward is that I would like to see contract extensions as soon as possible.

While the average remaining charter term is 5.2 years (with $850M in confirmed revenue), there are three vessels that will need new employment from 2028 on. While that’s of course still two years away, it will be a key element in determining what to do with the position. And also keep in mind two vessels are employed under charter agreements transporting LNG from the Russia-based Yamal LNG project to the buyers. Dynagas currently isn’t subject to Russia-related sanctions, but it also is an important factor to keep an eye on.

After Retiring the Series B Preferred Stock, Only the Series A Remains Outstanding

In the summer of 2025, Dynagas retired the Series B floating rate preferred stock, which means the Series A (DLNG.PR.A) is the sole outstanding series of preferred capital. This preferred share offers a fixed 9% preferred dividend ($2.25 per share, payable in four equal quarterly tranches of $0.5625 per share).

As the preferred shares are trading at a premium of approximately 5.5% to the $25 call price, the current yield is just under 8.6%. Given the low payout ratio versus the net income and the free cash flow, the preferred shares offer an excellent dividend coverage ratio, but keep in mind this series of preferred shares can be retired at any given moment. I don’t think this is a near-term risk, as creditors likely wouldn’t be too keen on the company spending $75M on yet another preferred stock buyback, but the risk cannot be ruled out, as DLNG also just announced a share repurchase program on its common stock. So while I don’t think these shares will be called in the very near future, it is a risk investors should be aware of.

Investment Thesis

At the end of the third quarter, the book value per common share exceeded $10, so while the stock isn’t very interesting from an income perspective, the good earnings visibility for the next two years should bode well for the share price. Of course it will be very interesting to see what will happen once existing charters have to be renewed, as we have recently seen competitors deciding to scrap older vessels.

If income is important, then the preferred shares could still be interesting. Although they are callable at any given moment, the distribution hike and recently announced common share buyback may indicate Dynagas is in no rush to retire its 9% yielding preferred stock.

I have a small position in both the common stock as well as the preferred stock. I would consider selling the common shares closer to $6-7 (60-70% of the book value, unless Dynagas provides more clarity on potential charters from 2028 on). At the current share price of less than $3.70, the stock is trading at just 1/3 of the book value, likely due to the uncertainty related to the fair value of older vessels, as well as the charter maturities in 2028 for three of the vessels. That’s why—despite the strong earnings and cash flows—I still consider DLNG a speculative long position. I feel more at ease with the preferred stock, but that represents a small position in my portfolio as well.

data.title: Carnival's Diversified Destinations Deliver Resilient Booking Trends - Rally Still Has Legs
data.description:
I previously covered Carnival Corporation & plc (CCL) in November 2025, discussing how the cruise company had reported the durable consumer demand for cruising, as observed in the raised FY2025 guidance and the management achieving the 2026 SEA Change targets 18 months ahead of schedule.

Combined with the excellent 2026 booking trends at higher prices, the growing customer deposits, the expanded investments in its new fleet through 2033/private islands through 2026, and the tailwinds from the new loyalty program, I had reiterated my Buy rating upon a moderate retracement then.

In this article, I shall discuss why I am reiterating my Buy rating for the CCL stock, thanks to its much needed return to "investment grade leverage metrics" and the consequently reinstated dividend payouts, significantly aided by the promising FY2026 guidance and the healthier balance sheet.

This is on top of the reduced exposure to the Caribbean oversupply risks, balanced by the robust booking trends in the EU, as observed in the elevated customer deposits.

CCL Hints At FY2026/FY2027 Outperformance

CCL 1Y Stock Price

Since then, my dip Buy recommendation had materialized during the worst of the November 2025 correction at the $24s, between the established intermediate support line at the $26s and the base support line at the $20s.

This was before the bulls defended CCL's recovery, significantly aided by the robust performance metrics observed in the FQ4'25 earnings call on December 19, 2025 along with the reinstated dividend after the last announced payout during the early days of COVID-19 pandemic in January 2020.

While the initial quarterly dividend of $0.15 per share payable on February 27, 2026 is relatively underwhelming compared to the last payout of $0.50 per share, I am not overly concerned indeed, since it already signals their return to "investment grade leverage metrics."

This is on top of the management's confidence about their "ability to generate sustainable cash flow, disciplined capital allocation strategy and strong financial position," while returning great value to long-term share holders, partly aided by the lack of new fleet delivery in 2026.

While CCL's return to investment grade is somewhat delayed compared to its direct competitor, Royal Caribbean Cruises Ltd. (RCL) in July 2024, I am of the opinion that it is better to be late than never.

This is especially since CCL now boasts a relatively richer forward dividend yield of ~1.8%, based on the current stock prices of $31.69 and the annualized dividend payout of $0.60 per share, compared to RCL at ~1.32%.

CCL appears more than well capitalized to maintain a secure income investment thesis, based on the FY2025 Free Cash Flow generation at $2.6B (+100% YoY), against the estimated annualized dividend obligation of $0.84B (based on the FQ4'25 shares outstanding at 1.4B and the $0.60 annualized payout per share).

Moving forward, CCL has already offered a promising FY2026 guidance, with a net yield growth of +2.5% YoY in constant currency (compared to FY2025 levels of +5.5%), adj EBITDA of $7.63B (+5.9% YoY), and adj EPS of $2.48 (+10.2% YoY).

When compared to the planned capacity growth by +0.9% YoY in FY2026, it is apparent that the management has priced in higher ticket prices and onboard spending per diem trends ahead.

With part of its FY2026/FY2027 capacity already booked at "historical high prices for both North America and Europe," aided by the insights offered by the higher customer deposits at $7.24B (+1.4% QoQ/+6.9% YoY), CCL appears well positioned to weather the industry wide headwinds in 2026.

For one, CCL (similar to RCL and Norwegian Cruise Line Holdings (NCLH)) already announces an enhanced loyalty program, with it expected to trigger a slight bottom-line impact on its net yield growth through FY2027 albeit with the added benefit of "improving customer engagement and increasing customer lifetime value."

Secondly, readers must note that NCLH has doubled down on their family friendly pricing, their expanded Caribbean capacity to over 50%, and the shorter sailings/excursions - all of which are expected to trigger sales headwinds to RCL, since "the Caribbean represents 57% of our deployment this year and 63% of capacity in the fourth quarter."

Fortunately for readers, CCL is less exposed to the Caribbean cruising oversupply risks, since the region only comprise 35% of its cruising capacity in 2026, aided by the robust booking trends in the EU, with the EU comprising 31% of the company's capacity.

As a result of the promising FY2026 guidance and the robust booking trends, I can understand why the CCL stock has been able to enjoy an outsized rally of +27.9% from the recent November 2025 bottom of $24s.

This is significantly aided by the healthier net debt to adjusted EBITDA ratio of 3.4x in FQ4'25, down from 4.3x in FQ4'24 and the peak levels of -18.1x in FQ4'22 (author estimated), while notably nearer to the 2.02x observed in FY2019 (author estimated).

This is especially since CCL appears well capitalized to deliver both shareholder return across dividend payouts, opportunistic share repurchases (18M shares retired as of FQ4'25 earnings call), and balance sheet deleveraging to the target ratio of under 3x ahead, thanks to the richer cash flows.

The Consensus Forward Estimates

Based on CCL's thrice raised FY2025 guidance after the consecutive double beat performance over the past twelve quarters (aside from the one-time FQ4'25 top-line miss by -0.6%), it seems that the consensus is pricing in another beat and raise performance based on the slightly higher FY2026 adj EPS estimates of $2.54 (+12.8% YoY) against the management's current guidance of $2.48 (+10.2% YoY).

Otherwise, I am of the opinion that the current consensus forward estimates appear achievable, with CCL expected to report an excellent top/bottom-line growth at a CAGR of +4.2%/+12.3% through FY2028.

CCL Valuations

Thanks to the ongoing stock price volatility over the past few months, CCL remains compellingly valued at FWD EV/EBITDA valuations of 8.84x and FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 12.63x as well, compared to the 1Y mean of 8.59x/12.62x and the 5Y pre-pandemic mean of 9.63x/14.50x, respectively.

Based on the projected recovery in its adj EPS at a 3Y CAGR of +12.3%, CCL appears reasonable at my estimated 3Y PEG non-GAAP ratio of 1.02x as well, compared to the 5Y pre-pandemic ratio of 0.78x, the sector median of 1.80x, and the direct cruise peers, including:

  • RCL at 0.96x (using the same methodology),
  • Viking Holdings (VIK) at 1.00x,
  • NCLH at 0.65x, and
  • Disney (DIS) at 1.51x,

with it apparent that the CCL continues to offer a compellingly valued recovery story despite the +110.2% rally from the April 2025 bottom and the +297.6% rally from the April 2020 bottom.

CCL Remains A Great Buy Upon A Dip

CCL 5Y Stock Price

For now, CCL continues to trade well within the established trading pattern since the October 2022 bottom, with it providing interested investors with a great insight into its future stock price movement.

Based on the 5Y pre-pandemic P/E mean of 14.50x (cheap compared to the peer group mean of ~17.98x) and the FY2025 adj EPS performance of $2.25 (+58.4% YoY), it is apparent that CCL is trading near to my fair value estimates of $32.60.

Based on the consensus FY2028 adj EPS estimates of $3.18, there is an excellent upside potential of +45.4% to my long-term price target of $46.10 as well, despite the recent bounce off the 200 day moving averages/support levels of $24s.

Combined with the reinstatement of the dividend payouts and the healthier balance sheet, I am reiterating my Buy rating for the CCL stock.

Caveats Linked To Buy Rating

For now, interested investors may want to observe the stock price movement for a little longer, since it is uncertain if CCL may be able to break out of the 2021/2025 resistance levels of $31s.

With the FQ4'25 earnings call behind us and two more months to go to its upcoming FQ1'26 earnings call sometime in March 2026, I am of the opinion that, pending further near-term catalysts, the stock is likely to lose part of its recent gains and consolidate nearer to the 50/100/200 day moving averages.

Combined with the potential selling pressure after the ex-dividend date of February 27, 2026, my reiterated Buy rating for the CCL stock comes with the caveat that interested investors wait for a dip buying opportunity for a lower dollar cost average and potentially, the expanded dividend yields of >2%.

data.title: AI Reset Is Complete; Tech's Next Leg Starts Here
data.description:
The market rotation is in full swing, but tech stocks aren't collapsing beyond what the current reallocation themes have already inflicted on these previous outperformers. Tech (XLK) as a whole has kind of struggled (relatively speaking) as the sector continues to face such rotation pressure, as the Dow Jones (DJI) (DJX) and S&P 500 (SPX) (SPY) less the Magnificent Seven continued on their gallant advance. Yet, to think that since tech comprises 35% of the S&P 500 and that the Magnificent Seven comprises >31% of the SPX's market valuation, can such a disconnect persist through early 2026 without the "generals" starting to regain their mojo and exert their clout again in driving the market's advance? In my opinion, highly unlikely.

The fears of the AI bubble haven't gone away, and in all honesty, that's also a good thing, isn't it? We want people to stay cautious amidst the AI exuberance, indicating that the market hasn't quite gone into the extreme FOMO phase yet (that usually precedes a bull trap toppish pattern), and there's also no letup at all when we consider how early we are still in the multi-trillion AI infrastructure buildout phase, with enterprises still nascent in their AI adoption phase (but gaining cadence with more moving into production). I'm sure many of us are keenly scrutinizing when this massive adoption wave could be occurring, so that investors can start baking in the monetization thesis that could be used to justify the unprecedented AI spending fervor that is happening right now, and not stopping anytime soon yet.

Despite the pullback, there is little in the way that suggests the AI trade is going to roll over anytime soon. Meta Platforms securing its nuclear power deals (META) to lower the risks of allowing power to become a key chokepoint in stymying the execution themes. Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM) just unveiled a robust December sales performance that shows just how resilient this upcycle has proved to be. How about OpenAI (OPENAI), which is reportedly raising more funds at a markedly higher valuation of $750B, despite facing onerous headwinds from Google (GOOGL) (GOOG) catching up? Or how about Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang (NVDA) just unveiled this week at CES that Vera Rubin is primed to go into volume production from the second half of 2026?

Hence, if there's really a deep slowdown that could throttle the market's confidence, it's just not showing in the market tape (price action). As seen above, the XLK remains delicately perched with higher-lows and higher-highs price action indicating solidly resilient momentum swings that favor an advancing cadence, and not something sinister underneath that could precede a deeper and more destructive reckoning that could send bulls sprawling for cover quickly.

In addition, even the insider buy/sell ratios are demonstrating that insiders are using the current pessimism (or underperformance) to ramp up their allocations to tech, and not fleeing. Do these insiders think that the AI bubble is about to implode from their actions? Surely not, right?

Therefore, if anything, I think the rotation themes will be short-term in nature, and not expected to be structurally "destructive" on the main AI growth and tech themes when these stocks pick up steam later.

Furthermore, how often do we get the sector more than nearly 10% undervalued, as assessed with Morningstar's sector fair value estimates? It's a notable departure from the past three years, when we consider the 2021/22 bear market decline and last year's Liberation Day capitulation that saw tech stocks falling into more significantly undervalued zones. In other words, this really does seem like an opportunity for us to get more involved, and not less.

Next, how often do we get NVDA printing a forward earnings multiple of just >26x, which isn't far from the tech median of 25.6x, and the AI King is expected to deliver free cash flow of about >$200B by FY2028, with margins approaching the 50% mark.

Nevertheless, I think the market is also signaling that they want to shift out of the Mag-7 thesis or the AI trade to less expensive sectors in the market. However, if being relatively undervalued is all that matters, then the setup will be easy peasy, right? All of us just need to press the "Sell" button on our core tech/growth holdings and flip it immediately and "nonchalantly" to these undervalued sectors and buy aggressively right now.

And if that's the case, I believe we will not see the tech-heavy Nasdaq insiders (NDX) (QQQ) taking advantage of the current consolidation to load up during this pullback, and we will also certainly see the growth stocks moving into a downtrend (but that's not what I've observed thus far).

Plus, Wall Street still expects the Mag-7 to continue printing highly resilient and visible medium-term earnings growth outlooks (>20%) for the next three to five years. Plus, I believe if there is a group of companies that possesses the massive wherewithal, the businesses, and the structural and proven ability to monetize AI technologies (while still minting robust FCF in their core businesses), I think it's hard to fathom that our best tech companies aren't right there getting themselves ready to capitalize and charge ahead as AI proliferates deeper into the enterprise, with more companies moving from the experimentation to production phase.

Now, obviously, I'm not going to paint all nice and rosy out there. President Trump's so-called "Donroe Doctrine" is causing foreign policy watchers much consternation, as they reconsider the long term ramifications for the US and its global peers, especially regarding the reaction from China. The short-term reaction has been relatively muted, as the Venezuelan situation doesn't seem to be boiling over or worsening right now. Yet, we must be prepared that heightened geopolitical tensions could affect the market's confidence, as the US becomes a less predictable anchor and "guardian" of well-established international norms, while also affording leverage to China and Russia to exert more clout and pressure in their regional spheres of influence.

These worries haven't started to pan out yet, but they are worth paying heed to. In this case, I think the bullish narrative on the defense trade should persist, especially since President Trump wants to lift the 2027 defense budget to $1.5T. How it will be adequately financed and whether it's achievable and the follow-on effects will be keenly discussed and deliberated in the coming months, but the market sentiments have already bolstered related defense plays. These developments could also take the wind and mojo from the dominant AI narrative, as geopolitical factors and influences become an increasingly pivotal setup, with the market plausibly marking down the AI premium that has been a signature of the tech-driven advance so far.

Nevertheless, the US unemployment rate has finally started to show signs of an abatement, having consistently crept up from the 2023 lows, dropping to 4.4% as of the most recent reading. Consumer confidence has also risen from a long-term low, suggesting that 2026 might not be as somber as some long-term bears could have envisioned.

While a January interest rate reduction is likely out of the question as of now with the economy staying incredibly resilient, the market is still pricing in two cuts in the next 12 months, corroborating a largely dovish Fed posture. Also, inflation rates are still expected to stay relatively in control, validating the strength of the current position, and mitigating unanticipated reversals from the Fed into hawkish signaling.

Therefore, I believe that as long as the specter of a recession isn't what the market is expected to price in, I believe the heightened multiples right now of >22x forward earnings can continue to be sustained, with overall earnings growth continuing on an upward trend through the rest of the year, and possibly longer.
